sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=19, col=48).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 10, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: BREAKTHROUGH! Clicking the red framed shape on the right panel "transfers" it to the left panel - the frame disappears and a red block appears at [24,18]-[29,21]. The old red block on the left disappeared. Both right-panel frames disappeared. The puzzle is about placing shapes from right to left.
- Plan: Check current state to understand what remains, then figure out the goal - likely need to place all shapes correctly on the left panel.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 9 of about 59 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see the current state:
- Right panel still has the red shape (node 5, R, 47 cells) and blue shape (node 20, b, 47 cells) - but WITHOUT white frames now
- Left panel has a new red block (node 11, R, 24 cells) at [24,18]-[29,21]
- The old red block on the left is now dark red (r) at [30,18]-[33,21] (node 14)
- The gray box with green block is still at [38,8]-[43,13]
Wait, the right panel shapes are still there but without frames. So clicking didn't remove them from the right, it just removed the frames and added a copy to the left.
Let me reconsider. The frames (white borders) disappeared. The colored shapes remain on the right. A new red block appeared on the left at [24,18]-[29,21].
Looking at the image, the left panel now shows a vertical red block next to the blue square. The right panel shows the red and blue shapes without their white frames.
So the mechanic seems to be: clicking a framed shape on the right "stamps" it onto the left panel at a specific location, and removes the frame. The goal might be to stamp all shapes onto the left panel to complete some pattern.
But wait - the red shape is still on the right panel. So clicking it again might do something different or nothing. Let me think about what the goal is.
Looking at the current left panel layout:
- Blue 4x4 square at [20,18]-[23,21]
- New red block at [24,18]-[29,21] (6x4)
- Dark red block at [30,18]-[33,21] (4x4)
- Gray box with green at [38,8]-[43,13]
- Gray box with yellow at [18,22]-[23,27]
The right panel has:
- Red shape (no frame) at [17,42]-[21,54]
- Blue shape (no frame) at [34,42]-[38,54]
Maybe the goal is to click both shapes on the right to stamp them both on the left, completing the pattern. Let me try clicking the blue shape on the right.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Clicking the red framed shape on the right removed its white frame and stamped a red block onto the left panel at [24,18]-[29,21]. The red shape remains on the right but without a frame. The blue shape on the right also lost its frame. Goal likely involves stamping shapes from right to left to complete a pattern.
Plan: Click the blue shape on the right to stamp it onto the left panel.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Click the blue shape on the right (center around row 36, col 48)
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 36, 'col': 48}])
print(f"Result: board_changed={last_action_result.get('board_changed')}, gameplay_changed={last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed')}, level_completed={last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")

# Check changes
before_lines = last_transition.before_frame.ascii.split('\n')
after_lines = last_transition.after_frame.ascii.split('\n')
changes = []
for i in range(len(before_lines)):
    if i < len(after_lines):
        bl = before_lines[i]
        al = after_lines[i]
        for j in range(min(len(bl), len(al))):
            if bl[j] != al[j]:
                changes.append((i, j, bl[j], al[j]))
print(f"Changes count: {len(changes)}")
# Group changes by region
left_changes = [(r,c,ob,ab) for r,c,ob,ab in changes if c < 32]
right_changes = [(r,c,ob,ab) for r,c,ob,ab in changes if c >= 32]
print(f"Left panel changes: {len(left_changes)}")
print(f"Right panel changes: {len(right_changes)}")
if left_changes:
    rows = sorted(set(r for r,c,_,_ in left_changes))
    cols = sorted(set(c for r,c,_,_ in left_changes))
    print(f"  Rows: {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, Cols: {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
    # Show sample
    for ch in left_changes[:10]:
        print(f"  {ch}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
